speaker: interviewer
text: --experiment block. Describe what you knew about it and what you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, what-- in, in Birkenau, I knew nothing about the experiments. I heard about it after the war. Because friends of ours who were in it survived. Her name is Greta Himmel. Her husband was also Polish. He-- after the war, they got married. And what they'd done on that woman, unbelievable. They could get no children anymore. And let's see-- only one I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another one is Betsy Stodel. She works for the Holocaust Center. You ever heard of Betsy Stodel? She was born after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her mother was in the experiment block. And she'd done a lot of work with that. But this one I know very good. She lives in Culver City.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know people who were working in the experimental block at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was a, a block all separate because they were doing all kinds of things on those women, you know? Even men, I heard. I was lucky not to be with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed as a barber while the transports were coming in. Where they came from, unbelievable. One day, I'm walking outside. I got a chance at the SS. And there, I saw Mengele walking in front. I never forget it.

speaker: interviewee
text: With hums and marching, women and children and old people walking behind me, I knew they were going to the gas chamber. And one of the SS-- 'Go away.' Because they're afraid I'll say something. So he came. He hit me in my back. And I walked back. And they went right to the elders. People went to the gas chamber not knowing what-- I knew not-- in the gas chamber was the Sonderkommando. We heard about the Sonderkommando who worked in the gas chamber. They were Jewish. They were pulling them out the crematorium to be burned-- put them in the crematorium, you know. And all the bodies were taken out by the Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss with the other inmates of the-- the commandos, did, did you discuss about them? Did you talk about them to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Jewish people-- well, with me, we talked about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, they're going. They said, we know everything. We're the most-- Yiddish. They were all Polish people. There weren't many from Western Europe. There was the Dutch and some from Belgium, from Greece. They didn't speak Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you converse with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German. I understand German very good. I learned it in the camp, you know. And it's not similar with the Dutch language. But there are some words, you think it-- but my wife doesn't understand a word of German. But I-- I'm good in language. I do the crossword puzzles in four languages. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about with each other in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, there was not much to talk about. We was only thinking of food, trying to get some food to eat because the people were dying, getting thinner and thinner and thinner. And then it was the end. And the only thing to be alive, you got to-- had to steal. I'm sorry. You had to steal. From the packages from the Hungarians-- what they brought in, we took it out there. If you were caught, you were finished. So thank god I was never caught. And my friend, Max Alderwereld, I gave you the address. He brought in a lot of food. He helps with a lot. And we cooked in the back of the barracks when we were in the, the night shift. And that's how we tried it. And then all of a sudden, in September of 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in came the-- oh, a lot of SS came in the [GERMAN]. They said, raus! Raus! Raus! And there we have to stand with a German with a machine gun standing there-- in 1944. There was sabotage done by a few women on the crematorium to blow up the crematorium. And we're standing there. And I'm standing next to Max.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think we're finished? He said, don't say anything. And he began to cry, and I also. Then all of a sudden, they packed in-- the Nazis. They went away. And we could go back to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three girls. They were captured. They were shot to death. And that was the last of the crematorium. It was blown up. I think it was-- it was the last of the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anybody who came in from transport from September to November came from Bergen-Belsen. I remember 120 coming from Bergen-Belsen. They were all Dutch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I spoke with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to come-- they didn't come to the [GERMAN], the [GERMAN]. They had to undress. And I spoke with them. And he said-- what did-- I didn't know nothing about Bergen-Belsen. They told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, your brother and sisters were in Bergen-Belsen. Where did they go? I said, all of a sudden, we didn't see them anymore. They were only two months there and they were gone. I didn't know that. So I thought they were-- I thought also they were dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- they found out, this 120 had ancestors who were from England. So they thought because they're from ancestors from England, they could be interned. Two days later, all 120 were shot to death, all 120, every one of them. And they were taken away by the Sonderkommando, put them in the crematorium to be burned. And that was the end of that. And then they stop at the crematorium. Anybody who came in was shot to death. And that went on till December, 1944, when the Russians were coming forward, getting nearer and nearer-- Auschwitz. I didn't see no Russians at all. Because then, in 1944, we were all called together. That was the end. It was just-- just after Christmas. It was 1945 already. Called together-- we all had to get in, in a line. They gave us a piece of bread. And there, with a few thousand-- oh, about 10,000-- we had to walk. We walked through the snow for two days. A lot of them dropped dead. We were thin already. [NON-ENGLISH] my friend-- and we were still a little strong. And that's when we came to Gleiwitz. In Gleiwitz, they put it in different barracks. I didn't see my friend anymore. He was there. I met him after the war. And I was there. In Gleiwitz, we were there for two days. And they put us in the open wagons-- with nearly 300 in open-wagon trains that holds about 35 cows. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was lucky to get on the side. They were-- you know, they were desperate. They were fighting one another to get on the side. In the middle, you're dead. You're standing-- you're standing on top of one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm on that side. And it's snowing, no food, no sleep. Three days, I'm holding my side. I couldn't take it no more. And they want to try to pull me away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went over the, the wagon. And you got those buffers. And I got on the buffers. And I escaped. I jumped. I didn't know where I was. When I jumped, I came on snow-- so like this-- so, snow. They-- it went in a turn. And that's when I jumped. It was going 80 miles an hour, I think. There-- I was there. It was daylight. But it was getting dark. So I start walking-- the whole night with the snow up to here in a forest. I didn't know. I thought perhaps the wolves would come. Or the SS would find me. And I'm walking near the hall. I'm crying, standing by a tree. And then I see a light burning about a half a mile away. I walk through the snow. And I come to that light. It was a little church. I go inside. I'm tired. I fall asleep. I'm awakened by the father of the church. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still had my uniform-- you know, the striped uniform, what we had. And he knew I was from the concentration camp. He wanted to give me something to drink, some coffee. You know, I tried. But I fell asleep every time because I didn't sleep in all those days. And then I'm awakened by the gendarme. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they tell me this is Czechoslovakia. Where I jump, it was Czechoslovakia. And they took me to the town hall. And there, I had to be seated. And they told me, we have to bring you to Budweis. That's a city in Czechoslovakia, Budweis. They put me on a train with two gendarmes. And they brought me to the Nazis' headquarter. I saw it with the, the swastika hanging out, the flag. And there, I come in. There, the head Nazi was sitting there, starts speaking German to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I told him I, I was desperate. I had to jump from the train because they wanted to kill me. Where do you come from? I said, from Holland. Ah, from West Europe. You're not Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wanted-- every time he thought I was Polish, I said, I'm not Polish. I'm Dutch. I speak English. And he spoke also a little English. And I told him how it all happened. He said, you're telling a story. But I don't believe you. He hit me in my face. And they put me in a cell. I was in that cell for four days, all straw on the floor. Once in awhile, they brought a little piece of bread. And no latrine-- with a barrel we had there. And then I had to empty it. There were about five in there. And all a sudden, they came in. They called me out. And there were two Gestapo-- we called at that time the 'Gestapo.' They said, 'Take him,' in German. I did not know where they were taking me. And I walked in, in Budweis. Between them, they took me to a station. I had to stand with my face against a wall. And that stop-- there was also the same wagons. Where I jumped, they were stopped. It was nearly empty. So many were thrown or died or were murdered. And there, I sat there for two days in the open. That was in February in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then it stopped in Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there, I was pulled out. And I was put in Sachsenhausen outside. I had to stand on appel. You know what I mean, appel? You had to stand like-- they asked questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go in no barracks. I had to sit the whole time two days outside. And people walking like this and all the paths, and boom, drop dead. In the latrine-- that's the WCs-- and there were all dead bodies laying there. And then after two days, they took me out with a few of us and put us on the open train again. And there, we went to Flossenburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when I heard bombing and bombing. I heard the-- the British must come have come. Or so that's in my mind. I was in Flossenburg for about two weeks. And there, I didn't do nothing. I only had to stand on appel the whole time. And they were walking up and down, the SS. They knew it was coming. Finally, they put me out. And they put me in a train-- like a covered train, you know, where they put the cattles in. This wasn't open. There, I sat in a train with quite a few of us, no food. And they were-- I heard shooting and everything. And the train was going and going. Three days-- we stopped. Then it went further. They stopped, then went further. And finally, we come at the-- where we're supposed to be. That was near Passau, that place. It's on the Red Cross papers. And there, we were taken over by the German police-- not the SS. They was taken care of the place. There, I was put in the barracks-- no beds. We had to lay on the floor. And then the next day, we had to go start shoveling trenches, you know, a trench. And then the SS came. And they start. There were Hungarian SSes also. They were also from the Ukraine. Oh, they were terrible, the Ukraines. And they started-- 'Schnell, schnell, schnell.' And then I went through with it all that time until I couldn't do it anymore. I got so sick. And that went on from the end of February to May. And I remember the end of appel, they had to bring me in the barracks. And I was laying on the floor all that time. I didn't know what was going on. I was crying. I had the pan. I threw it on my head. I was-- my food was like this and everything. And I was laying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, one of the SSes-- 'Stand. Who could walk?' Anybody who could walk, they took them with them. Does anybody speak English? So I was wondering, for why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say anything. I was too sick. I couldn't stand. I was fainting every moment. So they left with 17 of us in that barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] Then all a sudden, [SOBBING] I was awakened by American-- [SOBBING] American troops came in. And they awakened me. Because I fell asleep every time.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, do you speak any English? I said, yes, I speak perfect English. And he was so surprised. The first one, he said to me, what's a limey doing here? Because you know, an Englishman they call a 'limey.' Because I was brought up in England, I still had the English accent. I told him the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, Jack, don't worry. The Red Cross will be here in a half hour. They'll be here. They're going to take care of you. Because we have to go further with-- the infantry had to go further. Then the Red Cross came in, the nurses. And they talked with me because I'm the only one. But, oh yeah, in the meantime, he told me tell all the prisoners not to eat anything. Because prisoners went by the farmers who could walk and stole canned meat and all that and brought it inside. If you ate it, you'd die right away. Because they were weighing 60, 50 pounds. Two died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were eating that meat, you know, fat and everything. The body couldn't take that, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Red Cross came in, they built-- with tents, like a hospital tent, you know? And there, they put me on the stretcher. I was laid down.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still have the book from the rabbi who gave it to me. [CRYING] And he talked with him. And then the colonel came to me. He said, tell me. I want to ask you, Jack.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was put on intravenous-- three bottles here and three bottles here. And he said, tell me. If you could get a hold of those German children from the SS, murdered your children, what would you do? I said, if I had chocolate, I'd give it to them. If I acted in the same, I would just be as bad as they are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was surprised. And he said, Jack, you're a brave man. You're going to be transferred to the hospital. After six days there, I was transferred. And that was tragic. He said to the driver, who was a sergeant, American, this man has to be right away in the hospital. He has typhoid. I've got all the papers here still from all that time. And there, he said, when you see a Red Cross flag hanging out, you know it's an hospital. And that was in Passau, which was about a half hour from where we were. So he stopped at the first hospital that he saw with the red flag.

speaker: interviewer
text: He put me out on a stretcher. He put me in. He said, you're going to take care-- this is a man from the concentration camp. But all I hear was German. I didn't hear nothing else. I thought, are you Polish or that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was laying there for 24 hours without looking at me. All I saw-- wounded German soldiers coming in, wounded SS coming in. And they put me later on the fourth floor. And there I was two days without being fed nothing at all. And then I'm crying. And I look out the window. And I see two soldiers standing there by lampposts. The man next to me, the German, he gave me a piece of paper and a pencil. And I wrote down in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope it hits his head-- hit it right on his head. And he picked it up. Angels-- angels, they came up. [SOBBING] They start crying. He said, who could put you in this place? It's not for you. You're a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, they still want to murder you. And she was arrested. She was still a Nazi, taking care of the Nazis, you know, the, the wounded. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was brought to a big hospital. And I have a photo in the hospital. And it was taken by the nuns. After that, they left-- the German-- The Americans took it over, the medics and everything. And I have this medic, Bill Conruh, who took care of me, and another nurse, tried to feed me with tubes and everything-- I was too weak. I said, I don't want nothing anymore. I know everything is gone. I know what happened. It's no use living anymore. I'll go back to [NON-ENGLISH]. And they tried to talk me into it and this and that. After 10 days, they had to leave to go to another hospital. And then it was taken over by the nuns. The nuns were very good. He writes a big story about these nuns-- very good. They were so nice. And they told me-- he said, listen. You got to try to get strong. Don't die. Because if you die, Hitler's won another one. They told me they were against it. They were hiding all the time. And they took care of that hospital. And they tried to make me strong. So I listened to them. And then I got stronger and stronger. But in the meantime, the medic who went away came quick back after a week, after two weeks, to see if I was still there. He didn't see me. I was transferred to another hospital. He thought I'd died. So I brought to the other hospital. I was taken very good care of by Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there, I was fed. I was getting stronger and stronger. And then in September, they think-- you think you're strong enough to go back to Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they brought me to the center where-- what they call displaced persons. And that's where they brought me there. I lay there. I was fed there and everything. And then after two weeks, I was put on the Red Cross train going to Holland with Dutch people. But there was another part of the train who were arrested. They were Dutch Nazis who went to work there. And they were caught. He told me that later. So I was laying there and riding. And we come in Maastricht. That's a city in Holland-- Maastricht. I was put right away in the hospital there because I was sweating. My fever was high. I was still sick. And there, I stayed two weeks. And finally, they put me in the Red Cross train to go back to Amsterdam. I come in Amsterdam on Central Station. And there, I was put out. They put me in a wheelchair. And they had doctors sitting there, taking the notes, you know. It's my doctor, Dr. Fedder, who I knew, who was our house doctor. He was in Theresienstadt all the time. But he was saved. And he was picking up the survivors. He said, Jack, you're going to the Spanish and Portuguese hospital. We had our own hospitals, the Sephardic hospital. No, not that I'm Sephardic. They took everybody. And there, I'm in this hospital. They took good care of me. And they thought I had TB. So somebody came and want to talk. They had to put something on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one person came. He didn't know. He thought that my brother and my two sisters and sister-in-law knew I was living. I was only about 20 minutes away without knowing that I was living. They told him. He says, well, I just came. I saw Jack. My sister screamed. Is he living? Is he living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they told. [SOBBING] Then they came in. [SOBBING] We hold one another for nearly an hour, crying, crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: My brother and sisters were interned. What happened to them when they were in Belsen? They were born in England. They still saved their passports. The Swiss came in, the Swiss-- the International Swiss Red Cross. I hear you have people who don't belong here, English people and that. And my brother said to my sisters, now, listen. Where they say where your parents are? My parents were-- died in London. Don't say they died in Holland. Just say, in London. They spoke English, my sisters, thank God.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, they were transported to Liebenau. They told me the whole story. In an intern camp, they got their packages every two weeks from the International Red Cross because the Germans were living in London at that time were also interned. See, there were no military people. They were interned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the International Red Cross. There was the Geneva-- I forgot what that was. The Geneva Congress made that. So they went there. And there, they were there for a long time until the end of the war. And they went back to Holland. Nothing happened to them-- nothing. They went until they found that I was living. What about your parents? What-- how did you find out what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother told me. They were taken away. He went to the house. And he saw people be taken away to where we live. They saw that they were there also. He didn't know what happened to them. He didn't know nothing about Auschwitz or Birkenau and all that. He didn't know. Until he went in the hiding place, but it didn't help. And finally, they caught him. And he was arrested with my two sisters and my sister-in-law. And that's when he went to Bergen-Belsen. But thank God he had his British passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever get notification of your parents as to where they eventually went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later from the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz-Birkenau. They were put in the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as far as your wife Ella--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have that in the Red Cross. 1942, they were put in the gas chamber. I have all papers. They sent it to me from Germany and from, from Holland and from here also. The real Red Cross, what time, everything. And my brother and sisters stayed in Holland not too-- a while. And after nine months, my wife came in the hospital where I was, my present wife. She was liberated in April, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ravensbruck, I think. I've got all the names of the concentration camps. It's all down there. And she was transferred to Sweden to recuperate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Prince Bernadotte was the one who'd done that. You heard the story about Prince Bernadotte laughing? He, he was shot himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, all those Jewish girls-- and she worked for the Phillips Telefunken in German. She had to work with the Telefunken. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And her story, she knows it better than me. And she worked there. And then after nine months, she went back to Holland, strong again. She was in-- like, a sanatorium to get strong and everything. They spoke good Swedish. And she came to the same place where I was. She was on the-- she was on the third floor. I was on the second floor. I could walk. I walked upstairs. And that's where I got to know my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were liberated, Jack, how much were you weighing at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was weighing about 64 pounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were first taken, how much did you weigh at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was about 130. I was skinny. You can see in my photo I wasn't very-- and then after that, my sisters went back to England again. They live in London. And my brother went to New Zealand. He lived 21 years in Auckland. I just spoke with my sister. Now my sister is very sick of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cancer on the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the cancer-- she's got now one breast off. And the other sister's still good. And she-- they went to live together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share these experiences with your brother and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Then my brother went to New Zealand. 21 years, he lived in New Zealand. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was president of the Reformed Temple in Auckland. Magnin was his guest for nearly four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi Magnin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He died later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, let's go back a, a little bit to when you got off the train originally in Auschwitz. What did you see? What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: People walking like this. It was like that, you know? They called that-- we, we, we called them 'Muselmanner.' They were walking like that. And nothing else-- and then they were dead. Where you walked, you saw nothing else but dead bodies. Auschwitz was terrible. They were taken away in the crematorium. When the most-- who were alive who went to the crematorium-- sick and alive who couldn't do nothing anymore. That was their end.

speaker: interviewer
text: You shared that Mengele chose 17--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 17, yes. I said in my papers. Now, what happened here-- I get a-- I got a call from the German consul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to be a witness against Mengele? I said, I know Mengele hit me in the face but-- that time. I said, sure. Why? They-- you got a hold of him? No, we don't have him yet. But there's a chance we know where he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in South America. They sent me the papers. We'll pay everything. Because I had to show up. And we don't want you to be-- and then I went to testify at the German consul. I still have the papers. They thanked me. But they never caught him. They said he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think he chose you and the other-- 16 others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were blue-eyed. We were blond. That was the whole history for Mengele. You were-- you didn't look Jewish. I was so white. You remember 'The,' 'The,' 'The Boys'-- the movie, 'The Boys of Brazil'? He wanted to make-- that was in his mind. You've must have something Aryan in you. That's why-- 17 of us, so blond. And there were a lot of-- and not one of them was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of his reputation at the time, who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mengele, yeah, we knew that. I knew it. Mengele was well known. When he came in that day, everyone-- Mengele is coming. Mengele-- we knew. He was well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you fear that you were going to be put on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I wasn't afraid anymore. But I was safe. God saved me. I was put on the other side. And that's when I was transferred from the quarantine to Birkenau in Block 18. And that's where I was put in there. And that's when it all-- and then-- it won't go out of my head. [SOBBING] Nights, I, I dream and see me. I had so beautiful wife and so a fine family. [SOBBING] They were so beautiful children. And every time, my wife said, don't cry. But she cried herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nine at home-- I always-- and then I wrote in the why? I wrote an article in the why why, why would they want from us? Why? What did those children do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the war started, when we were taken away, I'm sorry to say the Christian people jumped in the houses to take the furniture and everything away for themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot came out were on the German side. After-- when the Germans occupied, you know, they were all collaborators.

speaker: interviewer
text: Collaborators?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, collaborators. It was one policeman who was well known by the Jewish-- always came by my father to get cigars. He bought cigars from my father. He became one of the big Nazis. But this [NON-ENGLISH], he was shot. After the war, they hanged him. We were so surprised how the people later-- but Holland was so free country before the war. Now it's so changed. There's so many Nazis there now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's so changed. Everything is-- they can buy dope on the street. It's a different country-- where my brother lives-- he went back to Holland. He retired in Holland. He lives in Bussum in the countryside. There's a Jewish home also for people who are getting old and that. But private-- he has his own house there.
